title: Operators
description: Arithmetic, assignment, relational, and logical operators — including the integer-division gotcha and pre/post increment.
objectives: Use Java's arithmetic operators and understand integer vs decimal division
Use shorthand assignment operators and increment/decrement
Use relational operators to compare values
Use logical operators to combine true/false conditions

sections.text: 1. What is an Operator?
A symbol that performs an operation on one or more values (called operands), such as adding two numbers or comparing them.
We already used one operator without naming it: the = in int age = 25; is the assignment operator. Java has several categories of operators — today we cover the most common ones.
2. Arithmetic Operators
5 / 2 gives 2, not 2.5! When both operands are int, Java performs integer division and throws away the decimal part entirely — it does not round. This is one of the most common bugs beginners hit.

(double) a temporarily treats a as a double for this expression. This is called type casting, and it's a dedicated topic coming up soon — for now, just recognize why it fixes the division here: if either operand is a double, Java performs decimal division and keeps the fractional part.
3. The Modulo Operator (%)
% gives you the remainder after division, not the quotient. It's extremely common in DSA later — e.g. checking if a number is even (n % 2 == 0).
4. Assignment Operators
Beyond plain =, Java has shorthand operators that combine an operation with assignment.
5. Increment and Decrement (++ / --)
++ increases a variable by 1; -- decreases it by 1. There are two forms: post-increment (count++) and pre-increment (++count) — they behave differently when used inside a larger expression.

This pre/post distinction only matters when the ++ or -- is combined with another operation in the same expression. When it's on its own line, like count++;, both forms behave identically. We'll return to trickier expressions once we cover loops, where ++ is used constantly.
6. Relational (Comparison) Operators

= assigns a value; == compares two values. int x = 5; sets x to 5. x == 5 checks whether x currently equals 5, and produces true or false — it does not change x. Confusing the two is a classic beginner mistake, and in some contexts Java's compiler will catch it, but not always.
7. Logical Operators

rows: AND — true only if both sides are true
OR — true if at least one side is true
NOT — flips true to false and vice versa

sections.text: We have not covered if statements yet — that's the very next lesson. Logical operators are introduced here because they produce boolean results just like relational operators do; you'll immediately put both to use once if arrives.
8. Operator Precedence (Brief Preview)
Just like in math, Java evaluates * and / before + and -, unless parentheses say otherwise.

sections.code:
int result = 2 + 3 * 4;   // 14, not 20 — * happens first
int result2 = (2 + 3) * 4; // 20 — parentheses force + first

sections.text: When unsure, add parentheses. They cost nothing and make your intent unambiguous to any reader (including future you).
9. Putting It Together

sections.output:
a / b = 3
a % b = 1
Is a even? false
Score: 15

sections.title: Tracing it
points: a % 2 == 0 checks evenness: 7 % 2 is 1, not 0, so isEven is false
score += 5 makes score go from 10 to 15

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Expecting int / int to give a decimal result — 5 / 2 is 2, not 2.5, unless one side is a double.
Using = where == was meant, or vice versa — = assigns, == compares.
Forgetting % gives the remainder, not the quotient — 10 % 3 is 1, not 3.
Assuming ++count and count++ always behave the same — they only differ when combined with another operation in the same expression.
Skipping parentheses in mixed expressions and getting surprised by precedence — 2 + 3 * 4 is 14, not 20.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does 5 / 2 evaluate to in Java, and why?
questions.answer: 2. When both operands of / are int, Java performs integer division and discards the fractional part rather than rounding. To get 2.5, at least one operand must be a double (or cast to one).

questions.question: What is the difference between = and ==?
questions.answer: = is the assignment operator, which stores a value into a variable. == is the equality operator, which compares two values and produces a boolean (true or false) without changing anything.

questions.question: What does the % operator do?
questions.answer: It returns the remainder of dividing the left operand by the right operand. For example, 10 % 3 is 1, since 3 goes into 10 three times with 1 left over.

questions.question: What is the difference between x++ and ++x?
questions.answer: x++ (post-increment) returns x's current value and then increments it. ++x (pre-increment) increments x first and then returns the new value. They only produce different results when used inside a larger expression.

questions.question: What do && and || do?
questions.answer: && (logical AND) evaluates to true only if both sides are true. || (logical OR) evaluates to true if at least one side is true. Both are used to combine boolean conditions, commonly inside if statements.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Java's arithmetic operators are + - * / %, and integer division between two int values truncates the decimal part rather than rounding, which trips up a lot of beginners. Assignment operators like += give shorthand for updating a variable, and ++/-- increment or decrement by one, with pre and post forms that only differ inside larger expressions. Relational operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) compare values and produce a boolean, while logical operators (&&, ||, !) combine boolean values — together these are the building blocks for the if statements and loops coming up next.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: One equals sign ASSIGNS (x = 5), two equals signs ASK a question (x == 5?). And: int / int always rounds toward zero by dropping the decimal, never by rounding up or down.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What does 7 / 2 evaluate to, and what about 7.0 / 2?
When do x++ and ++x behave differently?

codeExamples.output:
a / b = 3
a % b = 1
Is a even? false
Score: 15
